---
format:
  docx:
    toc: false
    number-sections: false
---

11 June 2026

Dear Editors of *Ecology Letters*,

Please consider our manuscript, "A flexible modelling framework for estimating thermal tolerance and sensitivity", for publication in *Ecology Letters* as a Methods paper.

Extreme heat is one of the clearest biological consequences of climate change, but ecologists still lack robust tools for translating thermal tolerance experiments into predictions under realistic exposure. Thermal death time (TDT) and thermal load sensitivity (TLS) models are widely used for this purpose, yet their standard analytical process remains a two-stage modelling framework: estimate a time-to-endpoint at each temperature, then regress those estimates against temperature. This workflow is intuitive, but it can frequently violate statistical assumptions, discard uncertainty, struggle with overdispersed and hierarchical data, weaken group comparisons, and perform poorly under experimental designs commonly used in the literature.

**Our paper provides a direct replacement and extension of current approaches.** By fitting a single Bayesian four-parameter logistic model to the raw response data, we show how the quantities thermal ecologists already use - thermal sensitivity (z), critical thermal maxima (CTmax), critical temperature (Tcrit), heat injury, and survival - can be easily derived from this joint model. Through simulations, we show that the joint model has far superior statistical properties compared to the two-stage approach currently used. This new modelling framework allows propagation of uncertainty, handles censoring and overdispersion, supports hierarchical designs and group contrasts, and produces posterior predictions under fluctuating temperature regimes (see @fig-cover-framework).

**A key contribution we make is the accessibility of these tools.** We provide the `bayesTLS` R package, all data and scripts, and a detailed online supplement and tutorial so readers can reproduce the analyses, apply the workflow to their own data, and extend it to more complex experimental designs.

**We believe this manuscript is a strong fit for *Ecology Letters*** because it addresses a broad conceptual and practical bottleneck in climate change ecology: how to move from heat tolerance experiments to prediction of extreme heat impacts in nature. We show how to use the `bayesTLS` R package and fit these new models to empirical data from diverse organisms, including brown shrimp, zebrafish, snow gum, and vinegar flies. We show that the same workflow applies across plants and animals, lethal and sublethal endpoints, counts and continuous proportions, life stages, sexes, and can accommodate hierarchical data structures and overdispersion.

**The manuscript is original, is not under consideration elsewhere, and has been approved by all authors.**

Thank you for considering our submission.

Sincerely,

Daniel W.A. Noble, Pieter A. Arnold, Shinichi Nakagawa, and Patrice Pottier

![Conceptual overview of the proposed modelling framework.](../output/figs/fig1.png){#fig-cover-framework width=100%}
